segments.title: HMS Dreadnought's 12-inch starting point
segments.summary: The guide uses HMS Dreadnought's 12-inch guns as the visual baseline for later battleship-calibre comparisons.
segments.body: The opening establishes the size of a 12-inch dreadnought gun by first giving a roughly 10-inch human-head reference. Dr. Clarke then describes the gun's bore as a foot across, making HMS Dreadnought's main armament physically legible rather than merely numerical. That concrete scale helps make later increases in calibre more intelligible than a list of nominal measurements alone. It also makes clear why the 12-inch weapon is the natural starting point for a guide to dreadnought-style capital ships.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces dreadnought gun calibres and identifies Dreadnought's 12-inch gun as the first comparison.

segments.title: The step from 12 to 13.5 and 14 inches
segments.summary: The Orion-class 13.5-inch gun shows that a seemingly modest increase in bore size still mattered in capital-ship armament.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke places the Orion-class 13.5-inch weapon beside the 14-inch class of guns to show how close the two look. He stresses that the half-inch difference is visually modest but still large enough to matter. The comparison is a useful corrective to treating calibre labels as interchangeable when describing battleship armament. This moment gives a physical sense of the incremental escalation in pre-First World War gun size.

evidence.note: The transcript compares Orion's 13.5-inch gun with a 14-inch gun and says the difference still matters.

segments.title: Queen Elizabeth's 15-inch calibre
segments.summary: A chest-width comparison conveys the imposing diameter of the Queen Elizabeth class's 15-inch main battery.
segments.body: The 15-inch gun of the Queen Elizabeth class is presented as nearly as wide as the speaker's chest. Dr. Clarke frames the comparison specifically in terms of shell width, connecting the bore measurement to the projectile that would strike a target. The visual comparison turns a familiar calibre into an immediate impression of the weapon's scale. It is a useful watch point for understanding why the 15-inch gun became such a formidable benchmark in British battleship armament.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the Queen Elizabeth class's 15-inch guns and compares the shell width with a chest.

segments.title: Colorado's 16-inch gun in human scale
segments.summary: The Colorado class's 16-inch guns push the visual comparison beyond a human head, illustrating the next jump in heavy-gun scale.
segments.body: At 16 inches, the guide identifies the Colorado class as the American example for the next step in heavy-gun size. Dr. Clarke jokes that two heads might fit within the gun's diameter, continuing the human-scale method used from the opening. The point is not a ballistic calculation, but a memorable sense of how quickly battleship main batteries grew. It gives viewers a practical reference for the American 16-inch calibre before the final jump to 18 inches.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the Colorados' 16-inch guns and uses a two-head comparison for their diameter.

segments.title: HMS Furious, Yamato, and the 18-inch extreme
segments.summary: HMS Furious and Yamato close the guide's progression with a shoulder-width illustration of an 18-inch gun.
segments.body: The final comparison names HMS Furious and Yamato as examples associated with 18-inch guns. Dr. Clarke places that diameter at roughly his shoulder width, preserving the same human-scale approach used for the smaller weapons. This is the guide's visual endpoint: the jump from the early dreadnought 12-inch baseline to the largest calibre in the sequence. Opening here shows why 18-inch armament occupies the extreme end of dreadnought-style capital-ship firepower.

evidence.note: The transcript names HMS Furious and Yamato as examples with 18-inch guns and compares the diameter with shoulder width.
